perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Connecticut, and not the law of conflicts.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Delaware, and not the law of conflicts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Connecticut to Delaware could create legal contradictions if the specific matter in dispute has unique statutory or case law interpretations in Connecticut that differ significantly from Delaware. For instance, trade secret laws, non-compete agreements, or intellectual property protections might have nuanced differences that materially impact the outcome of a dispute. While both states have similar legal frameworks, specific nuances can lead to different interpretations and outcomes, thus creating a possible legal contradiction if actions were taken under the assumption of Connecticut law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles and State-Specific Statutory Interpretations
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the jurisdiction to Delaware when the original contract was intended to be construed under Connecticut law could introduce inconsistencies because the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws dictates that the chosen law must bear a reasonable relationship to the contract and not violate fundamental policies of a state with a materially greater interest. If the contract was formed and performed primarily in Connecticut, switching to Delaware law may be challenged, creating legal uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Conflict of laws - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Law for cases in multiple jurisdictions Conflict of laws Preliminaries Characterisation Choice of law Conflict of laws in the United States Incidental question Judicial interpretation Renvoi Public policy doctrine Hague Conference Definitional elements Jurisdiction Procedure Forum non conveniens Lex causae Lex fori Forum shopping Lis alibi pendens Connecting factors Domicile Lex domicilii Habitual residence Nationality Lex patriae Lex loci arbitri Lex loci rei sitae Lex loci contractus Lex loci delicti commissi Lex loci actus Lex loci solutionis Lex loci protectionis Proper law Lex loci celebrationis Choice of law clause Dépeçage Forum selection clause Substantive legal areas Status Capacity Contract Tort Marriage Divorce Property Forced heirship Hague Trust Convention Enforcement Enforcement of foreign judgments Anti-suit injunctions Revenue rule v t e Conflict of laws (also called private international law ) is the set of rules or laws a jurisdiction applies to a case , transaction , or other occurrence that has connections to more than one jurisdiction. [ 1 ] This body of law deals with three broad topics: jurisdiction , rules regarding when it is appropriate for a court to hear such a case; foreign judgments , dealing with the rules by which a court in one jurisdiction mandates compliance with a ruling of a court in another jurisdiction; and choice of law , which addresses the question of which substantive laws will be applied in such a case. [ 2 ] These issues can arise in any private law context, [ 2 ] but they are especially prevalent in contract law [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] [ 9 ] [ 10 ] and tort law . [ 11 ] Scope and terminology [ edit ] The term conflict of laws is primarily used in the United States and Canada, though it has also come into use in the United Kingdom. Elsewhere, the term private international law is commonly used, [ 1 ] for example in Switzerland , the Federal Act on Private International Law (PILA) regulates which law should be applied when a dispute has connections with more than one jurisdiction. [ 12 ] Some scholars from countries that use conflict of laws consider the term private international law confusing because this body of law does not consist of laws that apply internationally, but rather is solely composed of domestic laws; the calculus only includes international law when the nation has treaty obligations (and even then, only to the extent that domestic law renders the treaty obligations enforceable). [ 13 ] The term private international law comes from the private law / public law dichotomy in civil law systems . [ 14 ] [ 15 ] In this form of legal system, the term private international law does not imply an agreed upon international legal corpus , but rather refers to those portions of domestic private law that apply to international issues. Importantly, while conflict of laws generally deals with disputes of an international nature, the applicable law itself is domestic law. This is because, unlike public international law (better known simply as international law ), conflict of laws does not regulate the relation between countries but rather how individual countries regulate internally the affairs of individuals with connections to more than one jurisdiction. To be sure, as in other contexts, domestic law can be affected by international treaties to which a country is party. Moreover, in federal states where substantial lawmaking occurs at the subnational level, notably in the United States, issues within conflict of laws often arise in wholly domestic contexts, relating to the laws of different states (or provinces, etc.) rather than of foreign countries. History [ edit ] Western legal systems first recognized a core underpinning of conflict of laws—namely, that "foreign law, in appropriate instances, should be applied to foreign cases"—in the twelfth century. [ 16 ] : 9–10 Prior to that, t
perturbation.justification: The change in the governing law from Connecticut to Delaware introduces a potential legal contradiction. The law explanation indicates that the choice of law must bear a reasonable relationship to the contract and not violate fundamental policies of a state with a materially greater interest. If the contract was formed and performed primarily in Connecticut, switching to Delaware law could be challenged. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions connected to a case, where the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law is used. The Perturbation Explanation emphasizes that differences in state laws, even nuanced ones, concerning matters like trade secrets or non-compete agreements, can lead to different legal outcomes. Therefore, changing the governing law without considering these potential conflicts creates a situation where the contract's enforceability and interpretation are uncertain, particularly if Connecticut law had a stronger connection to the contract initially. This uncertainty and potential conflict with established legal principles constitutes a legal contradiction because it undermines the original intent and creates a risk of inconsistent application of the law.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Upon termination of the Evaluation of the parties' relationship with respect thereto, and upon Discloser's request, Recipient shall return to Discloser all materials reflecting or containing any of Discloser's Confidential Information and shall not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Upon termination of the Evaluation of the parties' relationship with respect thereto, Recipient may retain all materials reflecting or containing any of Discloser's Confidential Information and shall not be required to return any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing the recipient to retain confidential information after termination, the modified text contradicts standard trade secret protection laws. This is because Trade Secret laws in most states (including Connecticut and Massachusetts, the states of the parties involved) require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which includes the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination of a business relationship.  Failure to require return could be seen as a failure to protect the trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51 defines misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes unauthorized disclosure or use.  Allowing the Recipient to retain the information indefinitely increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or use, directly contradicting the protections afforded by this statute. The changed text removes the obligation to return confidential information, thereby removing a key safeguard for protecting trade secrets under Connecticut law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sec. 35-51. Definitions. :: Chapter 625 — Uniform Trade Secrets Act (contains Secs. 35-50 to 35-58) :: Title 35 — Trade Regulations, Trademarks and Collective and Certification Marks (contains Chapters 620 to 625) :: 2005 Connecticut Code :: Connecticut Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Connecticut General Statutes 2024 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2005 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 Connecticut Code - Sec. 35-51. Definitions. Sec. 35-51. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means, including searching through trash. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, limited liability com
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original text by allowing the Recipient to retain confidential information after termination. This contradicts Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51, which defines misappropriation of trade secrets as including unauthorized disclosure or use. By removing the obligation to return confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or use, violating the protections afforded by this statute. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines misappropriation as 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The change removes a key safeguard for protecting trade secrets, establishing a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement shall be effective as of the first date of disclosure of Confidential Information to Recipient. Recipient's obligations hereunder with respect to Confidential Information shall survive the termination of the parties' relationship. The confidentiality provisions in this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement and the parties' relationship relating to the Evaluation.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Agreement shall be effective as of the first date of disclosure of Confidential Information to Recipient. Recipient's obligations hereunder with respect to Confidential Information shall not survive the termination of the parties' relationship. The confidentiality provisions in this Agreement shall not survive termination of this Agreement and the parties' relationship relating to the Evaluation.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the survival clause for confidentiality obligations contradicts established legal precedent regarding the protection of trade secrets and confidential information. Courts generally uphold the enforceability of confidentiality agreements that extend beyond the termination of a business relationship, especially when trade secrets are involved. This change could make the confidentiality agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the Restatement (First) of Torts is older, Section 757 provides foundational principles for trade secret protection, including the duty to maintain confidentiality. Erasing the survival clause negates the reasonable steps required to protect trade secrets after the agreement terminates, which is a core tenet of trade secret law as understood under Section 757.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established legal precedent regarding the protection of trade secrets and confidential information by removing the survival clause. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that courts generally uphold confidentiality agreements that extend beyond the termination of a business relationship, especially concerning trade secrets. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that erasing the survival clause negates the reasonable steps required to protect trade secrets after the agreement terminates, a core tenet of trade secret law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides no information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 leads to a dead end. However, the contradiction is still evident because the changed text eliminates a key provision (survival clause) necessary for the ongoing protection of confidential information, directly conflicting with legal precedent that supports such clauses.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Each party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of the other party's Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to such other party. Each party agrees that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach by it of this Agreement and that the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Each party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of the other party's Confidential Information will not cause irreparable harm to such other party. Each party agrees that money damages are a sufficient remedy for any breach by it of this Agreement and that the non-breaching party shall not be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: By stipulating that unauthorized disclosure will not cause irreparable harm and removing the right to seek injunctive relief or specific performance, the changed text limits the remedies available for a breach of the confidentiality agreement. This contradicts established legal principles allowing for injunctive relief in cases involving trade secrets and confidential information, where monetary damages may be insufficient to compensate for the harm caused by disclosure. This change significantly weakens the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 allows for injunctive relief and other equitable remedies in cases of unfair or deceptive trade practices, which can include the misappropriation of trade secrets. By denying the availability of such remedies in the contract, the modified text potentially violates the provisions of this law, as a court may still grant injunctive relief despite the contractual limitation if the conduct constitutes an unfair trade practice. While the contract is between businesses, misusing confidential information can lead to violation of 93A if it's used unfairly in the marketplace.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle outlined in M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, which allows for injunctive relief in cases of unfair trade practices. By stating that 'money damages are a sufficient remedy' and denying the right to seek injunctive relief, the changed text attempts to limit the remedies available, potentially violating the law. The scraped snippet from M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 explicitly mentions 'damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper.' The changed text attempts to contractually waive a remedy that the law makes available, creating a direct contradiction.
